title: Overspill Material Management
body: Overspill at Stage 4D and the Old South Ramp has generated seven separate risk assessments across 18 months — the mine geometry keeps creating it, and the cleanup operation itself is one of the highest-risk activities on site. Getting the controls wrong during cleaning is how you turn a maintenance task into a fatality.
keyPoints: **Day shift only, no exceptions.** Overspill cleaning requires visual confirmation of stability above and below — night visibility is too compromised to manage that.

**No Stage 6 operations while Stage 4D is being cleaned.** The upper bench is the fall source; simultaneous activity above means uncontrolled exposure below. The dig limit is 15 m from the edge, demarcated with white tape — if it's not taped, don't start.
**Geotechnical sign-off before the excavator moves in.** If there's any question about stability, you wait for Maaden geo — this is non-negotiable under LSR 7.
**Excavator tracks perpendicular to the high wall, tramming motors at the back.** This is your quick-retreat posture. Scale loose material first, work in horizontal slices — never undercut the face.

**Treat it as a routine, not a one-off.** Seven RAs, same locations, same hazards. The recurrence is the finding. Consider whether Stage 4D/Catchment warrants a standing procedure instead of rebuilding from scratch each time.
apply: With Stage 6 active and the south route becoming the primary exit as the entry ramp closes, traffic pressure around Stage 4D will increase — confirm the dig limit tape is in place and Stage 6 has been stood down before any cleaning work begins.
